title: South Carolina vs UConn — Women’s Final Four Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Women’s Tournament • Final Four • Phoenix, AZ
venue: Mortgage Matchup Center, Phoenix, Arizona • ESPN
extraInfo: Winner advances to the National Championship on Sunday, April 5 at 3:30 PM ET on ABC

stakes.callout: The most anticipated semifinal in women's basketball history. No. 1 South Carolina — three-time national champions seeking revenge for last year's title game blowout — faces No. 1 UConn, the defending champions riding a 54-game winning streak and a perfect 38-0 season. These programs have met in two of the last four national championship games. UConn won the 2025 title 82-59 and beat South Carolina 87-58 in the regular season. Dawn Staley's dynasty vs. Geno Auriemma's dynasty — with a combined 15 national championships between them — in a game that could decide who plays for the 2026 crown. This is as big as women's college basketball gets.

cards.label: Revenge Game: 82-59
cards.value: UConn dismantled South Carolina 82-59 in the 2025 national championship game, then beat them 87-58 in February. That's back-to-back blowout losses — a combined 52-point deficit. South Carolina has never lost three straight to the same team under Dawn Staley. "We have a lot of room to grow," Raven Johnson said after the Elite Eight. The Gamecocks have been growing toward this moment all season.

cards.label: UConn's Pursuit of Perfection
cards.value: UConn is 38-0 with a 54-game winning streak. A loss here would end one of the great seasons in women's basketball history. The Huskies have won every game by at least 18 points in the tournament. The last team to complete an undefeated season and win the national championship? South Carolina in 2024 (38-0). If UConn does it, they'd be the sixth team ever.

cards.label: Dynasty vs. Dynasty
cards.value: Dawn Staley: 3 national titles, 7 Final Fours, 680 career wins. Geno Auriemma: 12 national titles, 25 Final Fours, 1,288 career wins. These are the two greatest active coaches in women's basketball, and they're about to meet in the Final Four for the first time ever. Their teams have played in two of the last four championship games, splitting them 1-1.

cards.label: WNBA Draft Showcase
cards.value: At least six players on the floor are projected first-round WNBA picks: Azzi Fudd (No. 1), Ta'Niya Latson (No. 6), Madina Okot (No. 10), Serah Williams (top 10), Raven Johnson (late first round), and Joyce Edwards (future No. 1 when eligible). This is the most talent ever assembled in a women's Final Four semifinal.

styles.title: Paint Dominance, Defensive Smothering, Tournament Machine
bullets: 87.4 PPG with elite inside-out scoring: South Carolina shoots 50.9% from the field and scores 47+ points per game in the paint. Joyce Edwards (19.7 PPG on 58.5% shooting) is the engine, with Ta'Niya Latson (28 pts in the Sweet 16), Raven Johnson (5.3 APG), and Madina Okot (10.9 RPG) providing multiple scoring threats. The 3P attempt rate is low (22.1%), but they connect at 37.3% — and shot 10-of-14 from three (71.4%) in the Sweet 16.

Defensive identity is Dawn Staley's foundation: South Carolina allows 57.6 PPG with a 69.0 HHS defensive rating. Opponents shoot just 39.6% eFG%. The Gamecocks generate turnovers at a 20.9% rate and created 36 points off turnovers in the Sweet 16. Raven Johnson (SEC Defensive Player of the Year) disrupts the perimeter while Okot protects the rim at 6-6.

Madina Okot is a matchup nightmare: The 6-6 center from Kenya averages 13.4 PPG and 10.9 RPG on 58.4% shooting with a surprising 48.1% from three. She's a double-double machine who was critical after Chloe Kitts's preseason ACL tear. Projected as a top-10 WNBA Draft pick.

Tournament dominance has been overwhelming: South Carolina has outscored four tournament opponents by 165 combined points, winning by 69 (Southern), 40 (USC), 26 (Oklahoma), and 26 (TCU). Joyce Edwards had 24 points and 12 rebounds in the Elite Eight. They have scored 78+ in every tournament game.

Five players average double figures: Edwards (19.7), Latson (14.7), Okot (13.4), Tessa Johnson (12.9), and Raven Johnson (10.2). Dawn Staley goes 10 deep. Three players are projected first-round WNBA picks. The roster has been to six consecutive Final Fours.

styles.title: Best Offense + Best Defense = Historic Dominance
bullets: No. 1 HHS rating in the country (58.9): UConn has the best offense (124.9 rating) and the best defense (66.0 rating) in women's college basketball. They score 87.9 PPG on 52.0% shooting with 59.0% eFG%. The 68.1% assisted shot rate reflects elite ball movement. Sarah Strong (18.6 PPG, 59.4% FG), Azzi Fudd (17.7 PPG, 45.4% 3P), and Blanca Quinonez (10.9 PPG, 54.1% FG) give UConn three distinct scoring threats.

Opponents shoot 33.4% from the field — worst in the nation: UConn allows just 50.1 PPG. Opponents make only 27.0% of their threes. The Huskies generate 15.7 steals per game (18.8% steal rate) with Sarah Strong averaging 3.4 SPG alone. No team has scored more than 65 points against UConn this season.

Sarah Strong is the National Player of the Year: The sophomore averages 18.6 PPG, 7.6 RPG, 3.9 APG, 3.4 SPG, and 1.6 BPG on a .600/.419/.850 shooting split. She does everything — she leads UConn in points, rebounds, blocks, and steals. Named Regional MOP after 21-7-5-3 against Notre Dame in the Elite Eight.

Azzi Fudd is shooting at a historic level: After years of devastating injuries, Fudd is averaging 17.7 PPG on .489/.454/.951 splits. She's made 104 threes (2nd nationally) at a 45.4% clip. Against Syracuse, she hit 8 threes for 34 points. She's the projected No. 1 pick in the 2026 WNBA Draft.

38-0 and a 54-game winning streak: UConn hasn't lost since January 2025. They won the Big East by 20-0 and have won every NCAA Tournament game by at least 18 points. The bench contributed 20 points (Quinonez) in the Elite Eight. Geno Auriemma has 12 national championships and 25 Final Four appearances.

keys.title: Can South Carolina survive UConn's defensive pressure on the perimeter?
keys.description: UConn holds opponents to 33.4% shooting — the best in the nation — and generates 15.7 steals per game. South Carolina's 22.1% three-point attempt rate means they want to score inside, but UConn's length and athleticism make getting to the paint difficult. In the two meetings last season, UConn held South Carolina to 58 and 59 points. The Gamecocks shot 71.4% from three against Oklahoma in the Sweet 16, proving they can hurt teams from outside when open. If South Carolina's shooters (Tessa Johnson at 45.1% from three) can make UConn pay for packing the paint, the Gamecocks can stay in this game.

keys.title: South Carolina's interior size vs. UConn's switching defense is the chess match.
keys.description: Madina Okot (6-6) is the biggest matchup problem in this game. She's averaging 13.4 PPG and 10.9 RPG on 58.4% shooting, and she dominated TCU inside in the Elite Eight. But UConn can counter with Serah Williams (6-4) and Sarah Strong (6-1 with elite defensive instincts). If UConn switches and forces Okot to face up rather than post up, they neutralize South Carolina's biggest weapon. If Okot establishes deep post position early and South Carolina's 39.2% offensive rebounding rate creates second chances, the Huskies' defensive numbers will crack.

keys.title: Azzi Fudd's three-point shooting could blow this game open.
keys.description: Fudd is shooting 45.4% from three on nearly 7 attempts per game. She hit 8 threes for 34 points against Syracuse and had 13 points in the Elite Eight. South Carolina allows 37.3% opponent 3P shooting — respectable but not suffocating. If Fudd gets even a sliver of space, she can hit from anywhere. Johnson and Latson will need to chase her off screens relentlessly. If Fudd gets hot early, this could look like last year's championship game in a hurry.

keys.title: The Latson-Raven Johnson duo must outplay the Strong-Fudd connection.
keys.description: South Carolina's Westlake High reunion has been the Gamecocks' tournament story — Latson exploded for 28 in the Sweet 16, and Johnson had 10 points, 8 rebounds, and 6 assists in the Elite Eight. They combined for 63 of 94 points against Oklahoma. UConn's duo of Strong (18.6 PPG) and Fudd (17.7 PPG) is the best 1-2 punch in the country. Whichever backcourt pairing can impose its will on the game — controlling tempo, creating for teammates, and making the big plays in the fourth quarter — will likely determine the outcome.

keys.title: Has South Carolina been tested enough to handle a four-quarter war?
keys.description: South Carolina's four tournament wins came by margins of 69, 40, 26, and 26 points. The Gamecocks haven't played a close game since the SEC Tournament loss to Texas on March 15. UConn has similarly dominated — every win by 18+. The question is which team responds better when the margin is 5 points with 3 minutes to play. South Carolina has three players who were on the court for the 2024 undefeated championship and the 2025 title game loss. UConn has last year's championship experience. This is where coaching and composure matter most.

prediction.summary:
This is the best semifinal matchup women's basketball has produced in years — the No. 1 offense and No. 1 defense in the country (UConn) vs. the program that has been to six straight Final Fours with three national championships (South Carolina). UConn's advantages are clear: they're deeper perimeter shooters, they have the National Player of the Year, and their defense is historically elite at 33.4% opponent FG%. South Carolina's advantages are equally real: Okot gives them an interior presence UConn hasn't faced all season, their rebounding is ferocious, and Dawn Staley has more Final Four experience than any active coach besides Auriemma.

The key is turnovers. UConn's 18.8% steal rate can turn this into a track meet — which favors the Huskies. If South Carolina values the ball and plays through Okot inside, they can slow UConn's transition game and make this a half-court battle. But the two recent blowout losses loom large. UConn has dominated this matchup — winning by 23 and 29 in the last two meetings — and South Carolina hasn't shown it can match up with the Huskies' combination of Strong, Fudd, and suffocating defense. Expect UConn to pull ahead in the second half as Fudd and Strong take over.

phases.title: First Quarter
phases.description: South Carolina comes out with purpose — determined to avoid the slow starts that defined the last two meetings against UConn. Edwards attacks the paint for an early bucket, and Okot establishes position inside for a layup. But UConn's pressure is immediate: Strong gets a steal and converts, then Fudd drills a three off a Arnold feed. The defenses dominate. Both teams shoot under 40% as every possession is contested. South Carolina trails 18-16 after a tight first 10 minutes.

phases.title: Second Quarter
phases.description: Latson catches fire — she hits a pull-up jumper and a step-back three to give South Carolina its first lead. The Gamecocks' paint presence starts telling as Okot grabs two offensive rebounds in one possession for a putback. But UConn responds with a 10-3 run: Fudd drills two threes from the wing, and Quinonez scores inside off the bench. South Carolina turns it over four times in three minutes against UConn's pressure. UConn leads 37-33 at the half — the closest halftime deficit the Huskies have faced in a month.

phases.title: Third Quarter
phases.description: This is where UConn has separated from opponents all season — and they do it again. Strong opens with a driving layup through contact for a three-point play. Fudd hits another three off a screen. South Carolina responds through Edwards, who muscles inside for two baskets, and Raven Johnson drills a corner three to keep it within 6. But UConn's defense tightens: two straight turnovers by South Carolina lead to transition baskets. Strong adds a mid-range jumper and a steal-and-score. UConn leads 58-48 entering the fourth.

phases.title: Fourth Quarter
phases.description: South Carolina makes its charge. Latson and Edwards combine for 10 quick points, cutting the lead to 5 with 5:30 left. The Mortgage Matchup Center is electric. But Strong answers with a contested jumper, and Fudd — ice in her veins — drills a pull-up three that pushes the lead back to 10 with 3:15 remaining. South Carolina can't get stops when it matters most: UConn shoots 5-of-6 from the line down the stretch. The Gamecocks fight to the end, but UConn's combination of defensive pressure and clutch shooting is the difference. The Huskies advance to the title game.

projectedScore.rationale: UConn's combination of the nation's best offense (124.9 rating) and best defense (66.0 rating) gives them the edge in a game between two elite teams. The Huskies have beaten South Carolina by 23 and 29 in the last two meetings, and their perimeter shooting advantage (Fudd at 45.4% from three, Strong at 40.4%) should open up driving lanes against a South Carolina defense designed to protect the paint. South Carolina will make this closer than last year's title game — Okot's interior presence and the Latson-Johnson duo are too talented to get blown out — but UConn's depth, shooting, and defensive intensity should be the difference in a 9-point win.

players.name: Joyce Edwards
players.meta: F • 6-0 • Sophomore • AP Second-Team All-American • First-Team All-SEC
players.bio:
From Camden, South Carolina. The 2024 Gatorade National Player of the Year and co-MVP of the McDonald's All-American Game. Scored 24 points with 12 rebounds and 3 blocks in the Elite Eight against TCU — her third 20+ point game this tournament. Shooting 58.5% on the season. Won gold at the 2023 FIBA U19 World Cup. Widely projected as a future No. 1 overall WNBA Draft pick.
Read more →

players.name: Ta'Niya Latson
players.meta: G • 5-8 • Senior • Second-Team All-SEC • Projected No. 6 WNBA Pick
players.bio:
Transfer from Florida State, where she led Division I in scoring at 25.2 PPG. High school teammates with Raven Johnson at Westlake High in Atlanta. Exploded for 28 points on 7-of-11 shooting against Oklahoma in the Sweet 16. Traded being a team's entire offense for a chance to win it all: "The purpose is bigger than scoring points." Projected No. 6 in the 2026 WNBA Draft.
Read more →

players.name: Raven Johnson
players.meta: G • 5-9 • Sr. • SEC Defensive Player of the Year • AP Third-Team All-American
players.bio:
The 2020 Naismith National HS Player of the Year. Two-time national champion (2022, 2024). South Carolina is 106-4 in her three full seasons. Had 10 points, 8 rebounds, 6 assists in the Elite Eight. Shooting 40.6% from three this season — a career best. Finalist for the Dawn Staley Award, named after her own coach.
Read more →

players.name: Madina Okot
players.meta: C • 6-6 • Senior • Transfer from Mississippi State • Projected Top-10 WNBA Pick
players.bio: Her path winds through Mumias, Kenya, Zetech University in Nairobi, and the FIBA Africa Women's Basketball League before Mississippi State and now South Carolina. At 6-6, she dominates the paint on 58.4% shooting. Became critical after Chloe Kitts's preseason ACL tear. A surprising 48.1% from three shows her evolving range. ESPN projects her as a top-10 pick in the 2026 WNBA Draft.

players.name: Sarah Strong
players.meta: F • 6-1 • Sophomore • Naismith National Player of the Year • AP First-Team All-American
players.bio:
From Durham, North Carolina. The No. 1 recruit in the 2024 class. Leads UConn in points, rebounds, blocks, and steals. Named Regional MOP after a 21-7-5-3 stat line against Notre Dame. Scored in double figures in 49 consecutive games. She's only a sophomore — ineligible for the WNBA Draft until 2028, but scouts project her as a future No. 1 overall pick.
Read more →

players.name: Azzi Fudd
players.meta: G • 5-11 • Grad. • AP First-Team All-American • Projected No. 1 WNBA Pick
players.bio:
The ultimate comeback story. Played just 42 games in her first three UConn seasons due to two torn ACLs. Last year, she was Final Four Most Outstanding Player as UConn won the title. This year: career highs everywhere — 17.7 PPG on .489/.454/.951 splits. Hit 8 threes for 34 points vs. Syracuse. Passed on the 2025 WNBA Draft to come back. Projected No. 1 in the 2026 WNBA Draft by ESPN.
Read more →

players.name: Blanca Quinonez
players.meta: F • 6-2 • Fr. • Big East Freshman of the Year • Big East Sixth Woman of the Year
players.bio:
UConn's first player from South America. Born in Milagro, Ecuador, she moved to Italy at 13 to play professionally in the Serie A1 league. Played for Ecuador's senior national team at 16. Scored 20 points off the bench in the Elite Eight vs. Notre Dame. Her composure in big moments comes from competing professionally since she was a teenager.
Read more →

players.name: KK Arnold
players.meta: G • 5-9 • Junior • Starting Point Guard • Three-Time WI State Player of the Year
players.bio: From Germantown, Wisconsin. Compiled 2,458 points and 8 triple-doubles in high school. Three-time AP Wisconsin State Player of the Year. UConn's floor general — 4.7 APG with a career-best 54.8% from the field. Scored 9 points and 3 assists off the bench in last year's national championship game. Doesn't fill the stat sheet, but runs UConn's offense with precision.

funFacts.title: 15 National Titles on the Court
funFacts.body: Between them, Dawn Staley (3 titles) and Geno Auriemma (12 titles) have won 15 of the 45 NCAA women's basketball championships ever awarded. That's one-third of all titles, coached by the two people on opposite sidelines tonight.

funFacts.title: Auriemma Immigrated from Italy at Age 7
funFacts.body: Geno Auriemma was born in Montella, Italy and moved to Norristown, Pennsylvania at age 7, teaching himself English. He didn't become a U.S. citizen until age 40 in 1994 — a year before winning his first national championship.

funFacts.title: Quinonez: From Ecuador to the Final Four
funFacts.body: Blanca Quinonez is UConn's first player from South America. She left Milagro, Ecuador at 13 to play professionally in Italy's Serie A1 league, competed for Ecuador's senior national team at 16, and is now starting in the Final Four as a freshman. She also tried soccer, karate, swimming, boxing, dance, and modeling before choosing basketball.

funFacts.title: Fudd's Comeback from Two Torn ACLs
funFacts.body: Azzi Fudd played just 42 games in her first three seasons at UConn due to two torn ACLs. She was the No. 1 recruit in 2021. She's now the projected No. 1 WNBA Draft pick and shooting 45.4% from three. Her mother Katie was drafted by the Sacramento Monarchs in 2001.

funFacts.title: Dawn Staley: Only Player-Coach Naismith Winner
funFacts.body: Dawn Staley is the only person in basketball history to win the Naismith Award as both a player (Virginia, 1991-92) and a coach (South Carolina). She also won three Olympic gold medals as a player and carried the American flag at the 2004 Athens Olympics.

funFacts.title: Jonathan the Husky Bit Yale's Bulldog
funFacts.body: UConn's mascot is named "Jonathan" after Connecticut's first state governor. Jonathan IV bit Yale's bulldog on the nose at his first football game in 1949. Jonathan V couldn't handle crowds and would "roll onto his back and moan." Jonathan VII was a gift from the University of Alaska after UConn students donated to earthquake relief.

funFacts.title: Hootie & the Blowfish Started at South Carolina
funFacts.body: Darius Rucker formed Hootie & the Blowfish in 1986 while at the University of South Carolina. Other Gamecock alumni: Lindsey Graham (U.S. Senator), Ainsley Earhardt (Fox News), and sculptor Frederick Hart (The Three Soldiers at the Vietnam Veterans Memorial).

funFacts.title: South Carolina: 11 Straight Years of Attendance Dominance
funFacts.body: South Carolina has led the NCAA in women's basketball attendance for 11 consecutive seasons, averaging 16,437 fans per game at the 18,000-seat Colonial Life Arena. A statue of Dawn Staley was unveiled in Columbia in 2025.

funFacts.title: Three Olympic Gold Medalists as Coaches
funFacts.body: Staley won three Olympic golds as a player (1996, 2000, 2004) and coached Team USA to gold in Tokyo. Auriemma coached Team USA to gold in 2012 and 2016. Between them, they've been on the sideline or court for five Olympic gold medal victories.

funFacts.title: The Gamecock Is a Revolutionary War Hero's Nickname
funFacts.body: South Carolina's "Gamecocks" name dates to 1900, honoring General Thomas Sumter, the Revolutionary War hero known as the "Fighting Gamecock." The costumed mascot Cocky debuted in 1980 and was named one of the greatest college mascots by Sports Illustrated.

funFacts.title: The Westlake High Reunion
funFacts.body: South Carolina's Raven Johnson and Ta'Niya Latson were high school teammates at Westlake High in Atlanta, where they won three state titles. Johnson recruited Latson to South Carolina. In the Sweet 16, they combined for 63 of 94 points scored or assisted.

funFacts.title: 111 Straight Wins
funFacts.body: UConn's record 111-game winning streak (2014-2017) is the longest in NCAA basketball history, men's or women's. The current 54-game streak would be the sixth-longest ever if it continues through the championship game.

history.callout: South Carolina and UConn have met 16 times in program history — 14 in the last 11 years — and this rivalry has become the defining matchup in women's college basketball. UConn leads the all-time series, but South Carolina has won 5 of the last 8 meetings (before UConn's two dominant wins in 2025). They've met in two of the last four national championship games: South Carolina won 64-49 in 2022 (the first championship game loss in UConn program history), and UConn won 82-59 in 2025. Tonight is their first Final Four semifinal meeting.

timeline.text: South Carolina defeats UConn 64-49 in the national championship game in Minneapolis. Aliyah Boston wins Final Four MOP. It's the first championship game loss in UConn program history.
No. 1 South Carolina dominates UConn 83-65 in Columbia. The Gamecocks go on to an undefeated national championship season (38-0).
UConn blasts South Carolina 87-58 in Columbia — the Gamecocks' worst home loss of the Dawn Staley era.

UConn wins its 12th national championship, demolishing South Carolina 82-59 in the title game. Azzi Fudd is named Final Four MOP. It surpasses UCLA men's basketball for most Division I basketball titles ever.
The two dynasties meet in the Final Four semifinal for the first time. Both are No. 1 seeds. UConn is 38-0; South Carolina is 35-3. A trip to the national championship game is on the line.

subsections.title: UConn: The Standard of Excellence
subsections.content:
UConn women's basketball is simply the most successful program in college sports history. Under Geno Auriemma, the Huskies have won 12 national championships — more than any men's or women's basketball program. They've made 25 Final Four appearances. They've had six undefeated seasons. Their 111-game winning streak from 2014-2017 shattered every record. They've produced Sue Bird, Diana Taurasi, Maya Moore, Breanna Stewart, and Paige Bueckers — arguably five of the ten greatest women's basketball players ever.

The 2025 championship — UConn's first in eight years — felt like a renewal. After years of injuries, roster turnover, and questions about whether the dynasty was over, Auriemma proved the doubters wrong. Now he's back in the Final Four with a 38-0 team and a sophomore (Strong) who might be the best player he's ever coached. At 72, he shows no signs of slowing down.

subsections.title: South Carolina: Building Their Own Dynasty
subsections.content:
When Dawn Staley took over at South Carolina in 2008, she inherited a program that went 10-18 in her first season. Eighteen years later, she has three national championships (2017, 2022, 2024), seven Final Fours, and 680 career wins. The 2024 team went 38-0 — one of only five undefeated champions in history. South Carolina has led the nation in attendance for 11 straight years. A statue of Staley stands in Columbia.

But the 2025 national championship loss to UConn — 82-59 — left a mark. It was a comprehensive defeat against the program South Carolina has spent a decade trying to surpass. Tonight is about more than a Final Four trip. It's about proving that South Carolina belongs on the same floor as UConn. A win would be the biggest statement of the Staley era — beating the undefeated defending champions to reach the title game.
